Glossary About
Nineteenth Century

George Romanes

1848 – 1894

George John Romanes should not be read as a thinker with one frozen doctrine.

George John Romanes should not be read as a thinker with one frozen doctrine. His philosophy of mind develops across at least four connected projects: first, a Darwinian comparative psychology of animal intelligence; second, an evolutionary account of the emergence of distinctively human self-consciousness and conceptual thought; third, a metaphysics of mind and body that he calls “Monism”; and fourth, a late re-opening of theological questions, especially the relation between will, causation, religious consciousness, and the possibility of a cosmic mind. The theological material is not peripheral. It is one of the places where Romanes’ philosophy of mind becomes most explicit about agency, causality, the “self,” and the limits of scientific method.

The mature position that can be extracted from Romanes is a form of evolutionary, comparative, anti-Cartesian, anti-reductive monism. Mind is not an immaterial substance inserted into matter, but neither is it a mere by-product of matter. The mental and the physical are two aspects of one underlying process. Consciousness appears gradually in organic evolution wherever behavior becomes flexible, uncertain, and modifiable through individual experience. Human self-consciousness is not a miraculous rupture in nature, but a higher organization of mental life made possible by sign-making, language, conceptual ideation, and the mind’s ability to take itself as an object. “Soul,” in his scientific writing, is best understood as conscious psychical life, not as a separable immortal substance; yet Romanes explicitly refuses to let science deny the theological possibility of a human “spirit” or immortality, because those questions exceed the competence of comparative psychology. In his later religious notes, the same monism becomes open to a theological reading: natural causation may be the phenomenal expression of will, and the universe may be interpreted, though not scientifically proven, as the manifestation of a psychical or quasi-psychical principle.

1. Corpus and intellectual trajectory

Romanes was one of the most important second-generation British Darwinians, a close correspondent of Darwin, and a major figure in the early development of comparative psychology. Darwin gave Romanes some of his psychological manuscripts, including materials that Romanes later published with his own work on instinct and animal mind. Romanes’ major texts for this topic are Animal Intelligence; Mental Evolution in Animals; Mental Evolution in Man; Mind and Motion and Monism; the earlier sceptical A Candid Examination of Theism, published under the pseudonym “Physicus”; and the posthumous Thoughts on Religion, edited by Charles Gore.

The chronology matters. Romanes began with serious Christian commitments, moved toward agnosticism and near-materialism in the 1870s, then gradually moved away from reductive materialism and toward a more religiously open monism. His wife Ethel Romanes and Charles Gore both present the later Romanes as moving back toward Christian belief, but their testimony has to be handled carefully: Thoughts on Religion is posthumous, fragmentary, and editorially mediated. Gore himself says the notes were unfinished and should not be treated as a settled system of orthodoxy, even though he sees in them a movement from unbelief toward belief.

This means the best reconstruction is not “Romanes the materialist,” “Romanes the Christian apologist,” or “Romanes the animal anecdotalist.” He is better understood as a thinker trying to preserve three claims at once: Darwinian continuity in mental evolution; the irreducibility of first-person consciousness; and the legitimacy, under disciplined agnosticism, of theological interpretation.

2. Romanes’ basic architecture: subject, object, and eject

Romanes’ philosophy of mind begins from a methodological distinction. One’s own mind is known subjectively and immediately: I am directly aware of my own thoughts, feelings, and volitions. Other minds are not known in this same way. They are inferred from behavior, expression, movement, communication, and analogy with my own case. Romanes often calls this inferred mental life “objective” in the context of animal psychology: not because mind is literally an object like a stone, but because the psychologist studies it through observable signs.

This distinction leads to Romanes’ important notion of the “eject,” borrowed in part from W. K. Clifford’s terminology. An object is what is presented in outward perception; a subject is the inward self; an eject is another subject inferred by analogy. Other people’s minds are ejects. Animal minds are ejects. In his speculative theology, Romanes asks whether the world itself may be interpreted ejectively: whether the universe, known outwardly as a physical order, may also be the expression of a psychical or quasi-psychical agency.

This is crucial. Romanes’ philosophy of mind is not built from behaviorism alone, nor from introspection alone, nor from theology alone. It is triangulated between first-person awareness, third-person biological observation, and ejective inference. The same inferential structure that justifies belief in other human minds also justifies, with decreasing certainty, belief in animal minds; and in a more speculative register, it permits but does not compel a psychical interpretation of the cosmos.

3. What is “mind” for Romanes?

Romanes defines mind in the broadest empirical sense as the occurrence of conscious thought, feeling, or psychical process. In the subjective sense, mind is what one knows immediately in oneself. In the objective or comparative sense, mind is inferred from the activities of organisms. The comparative psychologist cannot directly inspect an animal’s consciousness, but must infer it from the animal’s conduct in relation to circumstances.

The distinctive mark of mind is consciousness, but Romanes does not think consciousness is easy to locate. It cannot be seen directly from outside. It must be inferred. For practical scientific purposes, he proposes a behavioral criterion: the best evidence of consciousness is “choice,” especially when an organism learns new adjustments or modifies old ones through individual experience rather than merely executing inherited or reflexive responses.

But this criterion is evidential, not metaphysically exhaustive. Romanes repeatedly warns that it fixes the upper boundary of what is probably non-mental better than it fixes the lower boundary of what may be mental. An organism might have dim feeling before it displays the kind of learning that humans can recognize. Thus he does not dogmatically deny low-level consciousness in simpler organisms. He argues instead that our evidence becomes weaker as organisms become less like us.

This gives Romanes a careful position: he is not simply anthropomorphic, but neither is he willing to practice what he calls an inverted anthropomorphism, in which humans refuse to attribute mind to animals despite behavioral evidence that would support such attribution in human cases. Radical scepticism about animal minds, he notes, threatens to become scepticism about all other minds.

4. Consciousness as gradual, functional, and evolutionarily continuous

Romanes rejects a sharp Cartesian divide between mindless animals and conscious humans. In Mental Evolution in Man, he explicitly rejects the view that brutes are non-sentient machines. He maintains that animal reason, inference, emotion, and volition exist in forms continuous with human mental life. The question is not whether animals have mind at all, but how far their mental powers develop and where specifically human faculties begin.

His account of consciousness is gradualist. Consciousness arises, or at least becomes scientifically detectable, where reflex action becomes complex, variable, delayed, and centrally coordinated. In Mental Evolution in Animals, he associates consciousness with cases in which nervous centers have to mediate unusual or competing stimuli, producing something like hesitation, selection, or adjustment. He admits that the causal connection between neural process and consciousness remains mysterious, but insists that in experience the two are constantly associated.

Romanes does not draw a hard line at the possession of a nervous system, special sense organs, or any single anatomical threshold. He even allows that sensitive plants or protoplasmic organisms may have some dim form of consciousness “for all we know,” although he does not claim this as demonstrated fact. This makes him neither a simple panpsychist nor a simple emergentist in the modern sense. His view is better described as cautious evolutionary psychism: consciousness is continuous with biological organization, evidence for it increases with behavioral plasticity, and its lower limit cannot be dogmatically fixed.

Instinct and reason are also continuous. Romanes defines instinct not as blind mechanism, nor as direct divine impulse, but as reflex action plus consciousness. Reason develops as instinctive or reflexive adjustment becomes more flexible, inferential, and modifiable by experience. He distinguishes inference broadly from explicit ratiocination: animals may infer practically without forming self-conscious propositions.

5. Animal mind, animal soul, and the theological boundary

Romanes’ treatment of animal mind is one of his most important contributions. He argues that the lower mental faculties—perception, memory, emotion, volition, practical inference—exist in animals and humans alike, though in different degrees of complexity. This was part of his Darwinian program: to show that mental evolution, like organic evolution, proceeds continuously rather than through absolute discontinuities.

Here the word “soul” becomes delicate. Romanes does not build his scientific psychology around a scholastic or Cartesian doctrine of the soul as an immaterial substance. Scientifically, the relevant object is “mind” or conscious life. But he explicitly acknowledges a theological claim sometimes made by Christians: that humans have an immortal “spirit” whereas animals have only a “soul,” or that human conscious life differs in a revealed supernatural respect from animal conscious life. His response is not to ridicule the claim, but to delimit the authority of science. Comparative psychology can neither affirm nor deny such a doctrine. If there is a revealed difference between human and animal spiritual destiny, science is not the source of that knowledge.

That point is important for extracting Romanes’ philosophy. He is a methodological naturalist in psychology, but not a metaphysical dogmatist. He refuses to let theology dictate scientific conclusions about animal intelligence; but he also refuses to let science pretend it has disproved theological doctrines that lie outside its evidential range. This is already the pattern of his mature “pure agnosticism”: suspend premature negations as well as premature affirmations.

6. Human distinctiveness: signs, concepts, and self-consciousness

Romanes’ Darwinian continuity does not mean he denies human distinctiveness. His major problem in Mental Evolution in Man is to explain the origin of “human faculty,” especially conceptual thought and self-consciousness, without positing an absolute psychological break between humans and other animals.

His central distinction is between percepts, recepts, and concepts. Percepts are immediate presentations or sense-based mental contents. Recepts are grouped, generalized, or practically recognized mental contents that arise without explicit conceptual thought. Concepts are general ideas consciously formed, named, and manipulated as objects of thought. Romanes treats the concept as the point at which the mind does not merely have an idea, but can attend to the idea as an idea.

Language is therefore not an accidental ornament of thought. For Romanes, sign-making is the mechanism by which conceptual consciousness becomes possible. He defines language broadly, not merely as spoken words but as the translation of ideas into signs and signs back into ideas. The human difference lies above all in the power of symbolic transformation: the ability to use signs as vehicles of general, abstract, and self-conscious ideation.

Self-consciousness is the decisive higher faculty. Romanes describes it as the mind’s ability to know that it knows, to make its own states objects of attention, and to predicate truth as truth. Yet he does not think self-consciousness appears fully formed. He distinguishes outward or receptual self-consciousness from inward or conceptual self-consciousness. Animals and infants may have a practical sense of themselves as embodied agents among objects; mature human beings develop reflective self-consciousness through the linguistic and conceptual objectification of mental states.

This makes Romanes a gradualist about human mentality, but not a flattening reductionist. He sees genuine novelty in conceptual self-consciousness, but he explains that novelty as an evolutionary achievement built out of lower psychical materials. Language is both antecedent and consequent of self-consciousness: signs help produce reflective awareness, and reflective awareness in turn expands sign use.

7. Mind and body: why Romanes rejects materialism

Romanes gives materialism its strongest case before criticizing it. He accepts the empirical force of brain-mind correlation: mental activity is constantly associated with cerebral activity; higher intelligence is associated with more complex nervous organization; brain injury affects consciousness; and the conservation of energy makes crude interactionist theories of mind moving matter difficult to defend.

But he argues that materialism fails philosophically. It can describe correlations between neural process and mental process, but it cannot explain why neural motion should be accompanied by consciousness at all. To say that motion produces mind is, for Romanes, no more intelligible than saying that mind produces motion by a miraculous insertion of energy into matter. The relation between “neurosis” and “psychosis,” his terms for neural and mental process, cannot be made intelligible by reducing one side to the other.

He also rejects epiphenomenalism, the view that consciousness is a causally idle by-product of brain activity. If consciousness were wholly adventitious, an unconscious automaton could do all the work that conscious agents do. Romanes finds that unacceptable because intelligent volition appears to be genuinely involved in adjustive movement. Consciousness is not an accidental steam-whistle on the engine of behavior; it is one aspect of the total causal process by which organisms adapt, choose, and act.

This is one of the strongest parts of Romanes’ philosophy. He accepts the empirical dependence of mind on brain without accepting metaphysical reduction. He sees the difficulty of interactionist dualism without concluding that consciousness is unreal. In contemporary terms, his position is closest to dual-aspect monism, with some affinities to neutral monism and panpsychist or cosmopsychist speculation, though those labels must be used cautiously.

8. Romanes’ Monism: one process, two aspects

Romanes’ “Monism” is the metaphysical center of his mature philosophy of mind. Its core claim is that mental process and physical process are not two substances causally pushing one another. They are two phenomenal aspects of one underlying reality or one stream of causation. What appears objectively as matter in motion appears subjectively as mind. Neither mind nor motion causes the other; rather, both are inseparable aspects of the same event.

He therefore rejects both crude materialism and crude spiritualism. Materialism falsely treats motion as the real cause and mind as secondary appearance. Spiritualism, if taken as interactionist dualism, falsely treats mind as an extra-physical cause injecting motion into matter. Romanes’ monism says instead: the cause is one, but it is known in two ways. Objectively it is neurosis; subjectively it is psychosis. In the absolute sense, volition and cerebral process are the same causal event under different aspects.

This doctrine also explains why Romanes insists that mind has epistemic priority. Matter in motion is known only as an object of consciousness. Mind is the only reality we know directly. Our knowledge of the external world is mediated through conscious experience. Thus Romanes is not a materialist who thinks mind is built out of matter as known independently of mind. But he is also not a simple idealist who denies the objectivity of the physical world. The physical world is real as the objective aspect of the same unknown reality whose subjective aspect is mind.

The result is a layered position. Scientifically, we study neural and behavioral correlations. Psychologically, we acknowledge conscious life as directly known in ourselves and inferable in others. Metaphysically, we deny that either mind or matter is ultimate in isolation. The ultimate reality behind both is not directly knowable as such. This is why Romanes’ monism naturally opens into agnosticism rather than dogmatic atheism or dogmatic theism.

9. Will, agency, freedom, and the self

Romanes’ treatment of will is where his philosophy of mind meets ethics and theology. In materialism, will seems to disappear into brain mechanics. In dualistic spiritualism, will seems to become a mysterious external force acting upon matter. Monism allows Romanes to say that volition is genuinely causal without violating physical continuity: the volitional act and the neural process are one event viewed from the subjective and objective sides.

He sometimes describes the self as the source or origin of mental causation. This does not mean that the self is an unconditioned ghost arbitrarily intervening in nature. It means that from the inward side, agency is experienced as personal willing; from the outward side, the same process is described in physiological terms. The will is not outside the causal order; it is the psychical aspect of causation as lived by a conscious agent.

Romanes’ freedom is therefore not simple libertarian indeterminism. It is closer to a monistic agency theory: a person is free when action flows from the self as the conscious-volitional center of the organism, rather than from external compulsion. He thinks this restores liberty and moral responsibility because the agent’s conscious will is not eliminated; it is recognized as the subjective aspect of the very causal process that physical science describes objectively.

There remains a real tension. If the self is part of nature, then its formation has causes; if it is treated as a first cause, its origin becomes obscure. Romanes’ late notes do not fully resolve this. But the problem is not a careless omission. It is the point at which his philosophy of mind becomes metaphysics of causality: what do we mean by cause, if the only cause we know inwardly is will?

10. From sceptical theism to pure agnosticism

Romanes’ early A Candid Examination of Theism argues that the traditional argument from human mind to divine mind has no demonstrative force. A subjective need for God does not prove God’s objective existence, and the fact that human minds exist does not by itself prove that they were caused by a supreme mind. The early Romanes also emphasizes that science may treat the “Mind” hypothesis as superfluous for explaining natural phenomena.

In the later notes collected in Thoughts on Religion, Romanes reconsiders this stance. He does not simply abandon scientific discipline; rather, he argues that his earlier scepticism overestimated abstract syllogistic reason and underestimated the full range of human evidence. Religious consciousness, moral experience, spiritual need, and the “experiment of faith” may be legitimate data for a pure agnostic, even if they are not reducible to scientific demonstration.

This is not a minor biographical detail. It changes the philosophy of mind. Religious consciousness becomes a possible mode of disclosure, not merely an emotion to be explained away. The “soul” is not simply the animal mind plus conceptual thought; it is also the human person as capable of spiritual orientation, faith, repentance, and relation to a possible divine will. Romanes’ famous note about a “vacuum in the soul” that only faith in God can fill should not be treated as a proof, but it does show that his later psychology includes existential and spiritual dimensions of consciousness.

His mature agnosticism is therefore not indifference. It is a disciplined refusal to make negative metaphysical claims that exceed evidence. A pure agnostic, as Romanes understands the position, must be open not only to scientific evidence but to moral, spiritual, and experiential evidence, if those are genuinely parts of human consciousness.

11. The world as eject: cosmic mind without premature dogma

Romanes’ “World as an Eject” extends his other-minds reasoning to the cosmos. We infer other human minds from outward signs. We infer animal minds by analogy, though with weaker certainty. Could we infer, or at least conceive, a world-mind behind the world-object? Romanes’ answer is cautious: monism does not prove Theism, but it removes certain anti-theistic objections. The physical world may be the phenomenal aspect of psychical causation, just as bodily movement in another person is the outward aspect of inward mind.

He is careful not to anthropomorphize the world-eject too crudely. If there is a cosmic psychical principle, it need not have finite personality in the ordinary human sense. He follows the thought, associated with Herbert Spencer, that the ultimate principle may transcend specific human mental attributes while still being symbolically conceived in quasi-psychical terms.

This produces a striking theological philosophy of mind. The human mind is not an isolated accident in a dead universe; it may be a finite expression of a more universal psychical reality. Yet Romanes does not claim that this is a scientific conclusion. Monism by itself leaves the choice between theistic and non-theistic interpretations open. Independent evidence, especially religious consciousness and moral-spiritual experience, must do further work.

In his late notes, he radicalizes the connection between causality and will. We know causality most intimately through our own activity and effort; therefore it is at least possible that all objective causation is, in its inward nature, will-like energy. Mechanical causation would then be the outwardly observable regularity of a self-consistent objective will. This is one of Romanes’ most important bridges between philosophy of mind and theology.

12. A systematic philosophy of mind extracted from Romanes

The following theses reconstruct Romanes’ philosophy of mind as a coherent system. 1. Mind is first known subjectively. One’s own consciousness is the primary datum. External matter and other minds are known through consciousness and inference. 2. Other minds are ejects. Human minds, animal minds, and perhaps even a cosmic mind are inferred by analogy from outward signs. 3. Comparative psychology is legitimate because mental life is continuous across evolution. The same broad faculties—sensation, emotion, memory, volition, inference—exist in humans and animals by degree. 4. Consciousness is not an all-or-nothing property appearing suddenly in humans. It emerges gradually with biological complexity, behavioral plasticity, and the need for flexible adjustment. 5. The practical evidence of consciousness is choice, especially learning through individual experience. This is not a perfect definition of consciousness, but a scientific criterion for detecting it. 6. The lower limit of consciousness is indeterminate. Romanes refuses to deny dim sentience in simple organisms merely because the evidence is weak. 7. Instinct and reason are continuous. Instinct is not divine magic or blind mechanism; it is reflex action accompanied by consciousness. Reason emerges from increasingly flexible and inferential adjustment. 8. Animal minds are real. Denial of animal consciousness is methodologically unstable because the same kind of analogical inference supports belief in other human minds. 9. Human distinctiveness lies chiefly in symbolic conceptual self-consciousness. Humans can form signs, manipulate concepts, predicate truth, and know that they know. 10. Language is constitutive of higher thought. It is not merely a tool for communicating ready-made concepts; it helps create conceptual and reflective consciousness. 11. Self-consciousness develops gradually. Infants and animals may possess outward or practical self-awareness; mature humans develop inward, conceptual self-awareness. 12. Mind and body are not two interacting substances. Neural process and mental process are two aspects of one underlying causal reality. 13. Materialism is inadequate because it cannot explain why motion should become consciousness or why conscious volition should be treated as causally idle. 14. Interactionist dualism is inadequate because it makes will an extra-physical source of motion and conflicts with the continuity of physical causation. 15. Monism preserves both physical science and mental reality. Every psychophysical event can be described outwardly as neurosis and inwardly as psychosis. 16. Will is causally real. Volition is not outside the causal order; it is the subjective aspect of causation in conscious agents. 17. The soul, scientifically considered, is conscious psychical life, especially as organized into self-conscious personality. The immortal or supernatural status of the soul is not decidable by comparative psychology. 18. Religious consciousness is philosophically relevant. It may be a genuine mode of evidence for realities that scientific ratiocination alone cannot settle. 19. The world may be interpreted ejectively. A cosmic psychical principle is not proven by monism, but neither is it ruled out by science. 20. Theology and psychology converge in the problem of will. If causation is ultimately will-like, then mind is not an accidental latecomer in the universe but a clue to its inward nature.

13. How theology modifies, rather than distracts from, the philosophy of mind

Romanes’ theological comments should be read as a continuation of his philosophy of mind by other means. They are especially relevant in four areas.

First, theology clarifies the limits of scientific psychology. Romanes’ science can describe animal consciousness, human conceptual development, and psychophysical correlation. It cannot decide whether the human soul is immortal or whether revelation discloses a spiritual distinction between human beings and animals. This boundary is not anti-scientific; it is part of Romanes’ disciplined agnosticism.

Second, theology deepens his account of causation. If the only causation known from within is volitional effort, then the external world’s mechanical regularities may be the outward face of a deeper will-like order. This does not prove Christian theism, but it makes theism metaphysically intelligible within monism.

Third, theology expands the psychology of evidence. Romanes’ late critique of his earlier scepticism is that he treated abstract reason too narrowly. A philosophy of mind must account for the whole human person: intellect, moral consciousness, religious longing, spiritual discipline, and transformative experience. Faith is not a substitute for evidence; it is, for the later Romanes, an experiment whose verification may occur through spiritual intuition and lived transformation.

Fourth, theology gives cosmic significance to self-consciousness. If the world can be read as an eject, then human consciousness is not merely a local biological anomaly. It is potentially a finite manifestation of a psychical structure present more deeply in reality. Romanes does not claim to know this as science. But his mature philosophy allows it as a legitimate metaphysical and religious interpretation.

14. Non-English reception and interpretive clues

The non-English reception is useful because it shows that Romanes was not received only as a flawed Victorian anecdotalist. In French, Spanish, German, and Italian contexts, he often appears as a mediator of Darwinian mental continuity, a founder of comparative psychology, and, in the German religious reception, a symbolic case of the scientific mind returning from materialism toward faith.

The French reception began early. A French translation of Mental Evolution in Animals appeared in Paris in 1884, translated by Henry C. de Varigny and including Darwinian material on instinct. A later French edition of Mental Evolution in Man presents Romanes as a disciple of Darwin applying selection theory to mental evolution and emphasizing that the animal-human difference is one of degree rather than absolute nature.

The Spanish reception also began early. Spanish editions of Romanes’ animal-intelligence work circulated in the late nineteenth century, including La inteligencia animal and La inteligencia de los animales. A Spanish bibliographic and historical note presents the publication as part of the Biblioteca Científica Internacional, a project intended to spread the positive sciences in Spain, and describes Romanes’ work as completing Darwin’s organic evolution with a demonstration of mental evolution in the psychical order.

A modern Spanish-language history of behaviorism and comparative psychology frames Romanes as a foundational figure who introduced comparative psychology as the study of mind in evolution by observation and phylogenetic comparison. It also repeats the standard criticism: his use of anecdotes and popular animal chronicles made him vulnerable to charges of anthropomorphism, later sharpened by C. Lloyd Morgan, Loeb, Uexküll, and more objectivist approaches.

The German religious reception is especially revealing but also especially tendentious. The 1899 German translation of Thoughts on Religion was published as Gedanken über Religion, with the subtitle presenting it as the religious development of a natural scientist “from atheism to Christianity.” The translator, Eberhard Dennert, places Romanes in the context of debates over materialism, Darwinism, Haeckelian monism, and agnosticism, and interprets Mind and Motion as a critique of materialistic accounts of mind inclining toward pantheistic or theistic monism. This is valuable as reception history, but it should not be treated as neutral evidence that Romanes had completed a fully orthodox theological system.

Italian reception likewise recognizes Romanes as a leading Darwinian disciple and founder of comparative method in animal psychology. The Italian psychology archive ASPI summarizes him as defending a continuous psychological ladder from unicellular organisms to humans and notes the Italian translation of Mental Evolution in Man as L’evoluzione mentale nell’uomo in 1907.

Taken together, these non-English traces reinforce three interpretive points. First, Romanes’ central European reception was strongly tied to Darwinian continuity of mind. Second, his scientific importance was often understood in relation to comparative psychology before later behaviorist critiques. Third, his religious writings were especially attractive in contexts where Darwinism, materialism, monism, and Christian apologetics were being publicly contested.

15. Strengths and weaknesses of Romanes’ position

Romanes’ greatest strength is that he takes consciousness seriously without abandoning evolutionary science. He does not deny first-person experience, but he also does not isolate it from biology. He sees that animal consciousness is an other-minds problem, not a mystical exception; and he understands that any method sceptical enough to eliminate animal minds may also threaten human social knowledge. This makes him an important ancestor of contemporary debates over animal consciousness, comparative cognition, and the epistemology of other minds.

His second strength is his anti-reductive psychophysical monism. Romanes sees the inadequacy of both materialist reduction and interactionist dualism. His double-aspect account allows neural dependence, causal continuity, and mental reality to coexist. Even if his terminology is Victorian, the problem he addresses remains recognizable: how can consciousness be real if it is inseparable from brain process?

His third strength is that he treats language and self-consciousness developmentally. Human thought is not simply added to animal mind by supernatural fiat; it emerges through symbolic capacities that transform mental life from practical receptual awareness into reflective conceptual awareness.

The weaknesses are also real. Romanes’ animal psychology relies heavily on anecdotal reports, and later comparative psychologists criticized him for anthropomorphism. The development from Romanes to Morgan’s canon and later objectivist approaches is a standard episode in the history of animal psychology.

His criterion of consciousness is also unstable. If consciousness is evidenced by learned adjustment, but may exist before learned adjustment, then the criterion is methodologically useful but ontologically incomplete. Romanes knows this, but it leaves him without a fully satisfying account of what consciousness is in itself.

His monism is powerful but underdeveloped. Saying that mind and motion are two aspects of one reality avoids crude dualism, but it does not fully explain why the one reality has these two aspects, how psychical causation relates to physical law, or whether the “unknown” ground is more mind-like, matter-like, or neither.

Finally, his theological reconstruction remains fragmentary. The later Romanes opens a profound path from will to causation to world-eject to religious consciousness, but he does not complete the system. The result is philosophically suggestive rather than doctrinally settled.

16. Final synthesis

Romanes’ philosophy of mind can be summarized as evolutionary monistic psychism under theological agnosticism.

It is evolutionary because mind is continuous across life and develops from reflexive sensitivity through animal intelligence to human conceptual self-consciousness.

It is monistic because mind and body are not two substances but two aspects of one causal reality.

It is psychistic because consciousness, will, and subjectivity are not treated as illusions or causal ornaments. They are among the deepest clues we possess to the nature of reality.

It is agnostic because Romanes refuses to convert either science or theology into premature dogma. Science cannot prove the immortal soul, but it cannot disprove it. Monism cannot prove God, but it can dissolve crude materialist objections to divine mind. Religious consciousness cannot replace evidence, but it may itself be a form of evidence when interpreted within the whole economy of human life.

The comprehensive philosophy of mind extractable from Romanes is therefore not merely a theory of animal intelligence or a Victorian curiosity. It is a serious attempt to think together consciousness, evolution, agency, embodiment, selfhood, and God without sacrificing either scientific continuity or the irreducibility of inward life.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
